data.title: Trump set for Davos; U.S. policy shifts, Greenland to take spotlight
Asia markets in red on Trump tariff threats over Greenland; Japan yields hit 4% as Takaichi dissolves parliament
data.description:
Asia stock markets were in the red on Tuesday amid a sharp drop in U.S. futures after renewed U.S. tariff threats against Europe reignited trade uncertainty, while a surge in Japan’s long-dated government bond yields tightened regional financial conditions, weighing on risk appetite across Asia’s equity markets. Though Japan's PM Takaichi’s call for a February 8 vote aims to secure a mandate for "Sanaenomics" stimulus, the announcement failed to lift Japanese shares.

Reports on Monday indicated that Denmark is increasing its military presence in Greenland as President Trump refused to rule out using force to secure control of the island. EU leaders are set to discuss countermeasures at an emergency summit on Thursday.

Gold prices rose to a fresh record near $4,690 per ounce on Tuesday, driven by strong haven demand amid renewed US-EU trade tensions.

Malaysia’s annual inflation came in at 1.6% in December 2025, compared with November’s figure and market forecasts of 1.4%. Food prices in Malaysia rose 1.5% year-on-year in December 2025. Malaysia’s trade surplus stood at MYR 19.3 billion in December 2025, almost unchanged from the same month a year earlier and surpassing market expectations of MYR 12.2 billion.

Japan (NKY:IND) slid 1.24% and fell for a fourth straight session on Tuesday to below 53,000, while the broader Topix Index slid 0.6% to 3,635, after Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said she would dissolve parliament on Friday and call a snap general election to secure voter backing for her spending plans and broader policy agenda. The Japanese yen steadied around 158 per dollar on Tuesday after facing heightened volatility in the previous session.

Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi announced she will dissolve parliament on Friday and call a snap general election on Feb. 8 to secure voter support for her spending plans and broader policies.

Market attention is also on the Bank of Japan’s policy meeting this week, where rates are widely expected to remain unchanged following December’s hike. 

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.30% to around 4,100 while the Shenzhen Component dropped 1.7% to 14,050 on Tuesday, erasing gains from the previous session, and the offshore yuan steadied to around 6.96 per dollar on Tuesday, trading close to 32-month highs after the central bank left lending rates unchanged.

The People’s Bank of China kept the one-year and five-year loan prime rates at 3.0% and 3.5%, respectively, in line with market expectations, extending an eight-month streak of policy stability. Both rates were last cut by 10 bps in May. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.11% to 26,392 in early Tuesday trade, extending losses to a fourth straight session.

India (SENSEX) fell 0.32%. The International Monetary Fund raised its forecast for India’s economic growth in fiscal 2026 by 0.7 percentage points to 7.3% in its World Economic Outlook report released on Monday, citing strong momentum. 

Australia (AS51) fell 0.69% to 8,840 on Tuesday, extending losses from the previous session.

The latest Melbourne Institute report showed Australia’s monthly inflation rising 1% in December, the fastest in two years, while strong household spending signals resilient demand, keeping the RBA’s inflation challenge intact. 

U.S. markets were closed on Monday for a holiday. In the U.S., attention this week turns to the Q4 earnings season, with results due from Netflix, Charles Schwab, Johnson & Johnson, Intel, and Visa, among others.

U.S. stock futures fell on Tuesday as Wall Street reopened after the holiday-extended weekend, pressured by President Donald Trump’s threat to impose new tariffs on European countries opposing his proposal to purchase Greenland: Dow -0.89%; S&P 500 -0.96%; Nasdaq -1.09%.

Meanwhile, investors are turning to key US data due this week, including the PCE price indices, another estimate of Q3 GDP, S&P PMIs, and the University of Michigan consumer sentiment survey.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia markets and economy:

data.title: China holds key lending rates steady, extending eight-month policy freeze
data.description:
The People’s Bank of China kept the one-year and five-year loan prime rates at 3.0% and 3.5%, respectively, in line with market expectations, extending an eight-month streak of policy stability.

Both rates were last cut by 10 bps in May. 

Last Thursday, the PBoC announced cuts to sector-specific interest rates of 25 bps, effective January 19, to provide an early boost to the economy. 

Data released Monday showed China’s economy expanded 4.5% year-on-year in Q4 2025, the slowest pace in three years, though full-year growth reached 5%, meeting the government’s target.

On Tuesday, the Shanghai Composite fell 0.3% to around 4,100 while the Shenzhen Component dropped 1.7% to 14,050 on Tuesday, erasing gains from the previous session, and the offshore yuan steadied to around 6.96 per dollar on Tuesday, trading close to 32-month highs after the central bank left lending rates unchanged.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSE:TDF), (NYSEARCA:CHIQ), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:KBA), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:CWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:KURE).

Currency: (CNY:USD)

data.title: Denmark sends additional troops to Greenland
data.description:
Denmark has deployed additional military forces to Greenland after President Donald Trump refused to rule out using force to seize control of the Arctic island.

Several aircraft carrying Danish troops and military equipment landed in Greenland on Monday, with soldiers and the head of Denmark’s army arriving in the Greenlandic capital Nuuk and Kangerlussuaq, the FT reported. The deployment adds to more than 200 troops already stationed in the autonomous territory.

The move follows Trump’s threat to impose additional 10% tariffs on eight nations --including Germany, France, and the UK -- that participated in a multinational military exercise led by Danish soldiers on the island over the weekend.

When asked in an NBC interview whether he would use force to acquire Greenland, Trump responded: “No comment.” Asked if he would follow through on tariff threats against Europe, he said: “I will, 100 percent.”

The U.S. president’s aggressive stance has sparked fears of a trade war with Europe and plunged transatlantic relations into their deepest crisis in decades. Germany and France have called for a “clear” European response, with German finance minister Lars Klingbeil declaring: “We will not be blackmailed.”

EU officials have prepared detailed retaliation options, including potential levies on €93 billion of US goods. However, they are holding back their most potent measure  -- the anti-coercion instrument that could limit US technology companies’ access to the EU market, known as the "bazooka" -- hoping for a diplomatic solution during talks at the World Economic Forum in Davos.

Europe ETFs; (VGK), (EZU), (BBEU), (DBEF), (IEUR), (FEZ), (EWU), (HEDJ), (IEV)

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: China’s birth rate plummets to historic low despite government incentives
data.description:
China’s birth rate has fallen to its lowest level since the Communist Party took power in 1949, according to recent government data reported by the BBC and other media outlets.

Official figures released Monday revealed the country’s birth rate dropped to just 5.63 per 1,000 people in 2025, while the death rate climbed to 8.04 per 1,000 -- the highest since 1968. The result: China’s population shrank by 3.39 million, marking the fourth consecutive year of decline and bringing the total population to 1.4 billion.

The demographic downturn comes despite Beijing’s increasingly aggressive efforts to reverse the trend. Since abandoning its longstanding one-child policy in 2016 and later expanding limits to three children per couple in 2021, authorities have introduced various incentives including cash payments of 3,600 yuan (approximately $500) per child under three. Some provinces have implemented additional bonuses and extended maternity leave.

More controversial measures have also emerged, including a new 13% tax on contraceptives -- a policy that has sparked concerns about unwanted pregnancies and rising HIV rates.

Yet these efforts appear to be falling flat. China now ranks among countries with the world’s lowest fertility rates, at approximately one birth per woman -- well below the 2.1 replacement rate. Media reports indicate that high child-rearing costs and shifting lifestyle priorities are key factors behind young people’s reluctance to start families.

The demographic shift carries significant economic implications for the world’s second-largest economy. Experts warn of a declining workforce, weakening consumer sentiment, and mounting pressure on pension systems struggling to support a rapidly aging population. United Nations projections suggest China could lose more than half its current population by 2100.

data.title: U.S. Dollar eases as political tensions keep focus on Fed: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar was soft on Monday, retreating as investors reacted to renewed political tensions and shifting risk sentiment at the start of the week.

The greenback was last down 0.36% at $99.04, although the dollar index remains up 0.86% so far this year.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), which measures the dollar against a basket of six major currencies, rose 0.19%, as stronger U.S. economic data helped the currency register another weekly gain despite early-week weakness.

 

Political developments remained a key driver, with several reports linking renewed dollar volatility to investigations involving Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell and a broader debate over Fed independence. Standard Chartered described the move as a “buy on the subpoena, sell on the indictment” trade, while markets weighed the risk that political pressure on the central bank could lift risk premia.

Elsewhere, regional factors shaped major currencies. The yen weakened to multi-month lows on talk of a snap Japanese election, while several banks questioned the sustainability of long USD/JPY positions.

In Europe, sterling found support ahead of key UK data releases, while Bank of America and UBS reiterated a bullish medium-term view on the euro. In emerging markets, attention turned to Venezuela’s plans to resume dollar sales and to signs the Chinese central bank may reverse recent FX measures as the yuan strengthened.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose five points to 4.23%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) climbed four points to 3.59%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (January 12 to January 18)

Euro (EUR:USD) -0.28%
Pound Sterling (GBP:USD) -0.58%
Japanese Yen (JPY:USD) +0.63%
Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD) +0.18%
Swiss Franc (CHF:USD) -0.14%
Australian Dollar (AUD:USD) -0.28%
Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD) -0.18%

The Euro (EUR:USD) was last up 0.40%, finding support as euro zone inflation fell to 1.9% in December and Germany’s inflation eased to 1.8%, reinforcing expectations of softer price pressures across the bloc.

European markets were mixed amid a heavy run of economic data and renewed U.S. tariff threats, with investors weighing weaker trade data against signs of stabilizing growth in Germany and record highs in regional equity indexes earlier in the week.

The British pound (GBP:USD)  was last up 0.34%, supported after data showed the UK economy expanded 0.3% in November.

Sterling traded in line with mixed European equities as markets weighed softer U.S. inflation and renewed U.S. tariff threats.

The Japanese yen (JPY:USD) was last up 0.32%, firming as Asian markets reacted to softer U.S. inflation and mixed regional equity moves. The currency traded alongside fluctuating risk sentiment as investors weighed Wall Street’s tech retreat, political turmoil in Japan, and broader geopolitical tensions across the region.

The Chinese yuan  (CNY:USD) was last up 0.17%, firming as Asian markets tracked Wall Street moves and reacted to softer U.S. inflation data.

The currency found support after data showed China posted a record $1.2 trillion trade surplus, even as regional markets remained sensitive to geopolitical tensions and U.S. tariff risks.

data.title: Digital Realty Trust enters Malaysia through CSF Advisers acquisition
data.description:
Digital Realty Trust (DLR) on Monday announced an entry into Malaysia through the acquisition of CSF Advisers, a Greater Kuala Lumpur-based data center hub.

The expansion into Malaysia builds on the data center REIT's established Southeast Asia presence in Singapore and Jakarta.

CSF Advisers-owned TelcoHub 1 is an operational 1.5 megawatt data center.

In conjunction with TelcoHub 1, Digital Realty has also agreed to acquire adjacent land that can support up to 14 megawatts of IT load.

The acquisitions are expected to close in the first half of 2026.
